``` ::: ::: :::
:::: {.panel-tabset .nav-pills} ## Parole Eligibility
::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

How is Parole Release Eligibility Determined?

For most felonies, prisoners become eligible for discretionary parole release after they have served 50 percent of the sentence imposed by the court. This eligibility formula assumes that a prisoner has earned all available good time credits (see below) of 15 days per month; release eligibility can be postponed due to misconduct during incarceration. The period to parole eligibility can be shortened below 50 percent by the award of earned time credits (see below) of up to 10 days per month. Release eligibility for offenders serving consecutive prison terms is calculated as though the consecutive terms were one continuous sentence.


People who were Eligible for Parole

In 2020, White, non-Hispanic people constituted the most number of people eligible for parole but still in prison, accounting for 46% (1,296 people) of the parole-eligible prison population.



In 2020, there were 468 people who were incarcerated for a parole revocation and were eligible for parole but not yet released from prison.

``` ::: :::
::: {.cell} :::
## Prison Population
::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

Prison Population

In 2020, there were 2,828 people who were eligible for parole but not released from prison, constituting 17% of the parole-eligible prison population.




``` ::: :::

## Offenses ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

Most Serious Sentenced Offense

In 2020, there were 1,484 people who were parole eligible but still in prison for non-violent offenses, accounting for 52% of the parole-eligible prison population.

``` ::: ::: ::: {.panel-tabset} ### Overview ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
